data.title: WhiteFiber: AI Craze In Full Swing
data.description:
Shares of WhiteFiber (NASDAQ:WYFI) have seen a poor public offering. The AI infrastructure provider timed its public offering well, and while shares initially enjoyed a 50% jump, they ended up actually down slightly on the first day of trading, as investors apparently had some questions about this offering.

The company offers GPU infrastructure through data centers, essentially a supplier or landlord of such data center space + GPUs, as investors likely fear the set-up of paying an AI momentum premium, for what seems to be a somewhat commodity supplier of such services. Amidst many moving targets, I am quite cautious here, but so is the market.

AI Infrastructure

WhiteFiber is a provider of AI infrastructure solutions. The company operates high-performance computing "HPC" data centers and cloud-based HPC graphic processing units "GPUs" for customers to run AI applications and machine learning applications.

Trends have been plentiful and recent. The company was a subsidiary of a company called Bit Digital (BTBT) and as recently as October last year it acquired Enovum, a Montreal-based HPC data center with a 4 MW AI datacenter, now having 14 customers who fully occupy this facility for an average of about 30 months. Later that year, the company acquired real estate and buildings for another 5 MW AI center in Montreal, and another 7 MW building in Quebec in April of this year.

In May, the company bought a big existing industrial building in North Carolina, to grow up to 99 MW in the coming four years, as the company claims that it has a pipeline of some 800 MW of new projects under non-binding and exclusive LOIs. As of June, the company has 4,500 NVIDIA GPUs deployed, with another 4,000 under contract.

The company furthermore indicates that capital expenditures related to tier-3 data centers come in at $7-$9 million per gross MW, that is, excluding about a million for targeted real estate as well. With these costs running near $10 million per MW, the pipeline of 800 MW would eventually require $8 billion in financing to become realised. Much of this ought to be financed with facility debt, as the company targets impressive EBITDA and equity returns.

Valuation and IPO Thoughts

WhiteFiber aimed to sell 7.8 million shares in a preliminary price range between $15 and $17 per share, with final pricing set at $17 per share, at the high end of the midpoint of the preliminary guidance. This means that the business obtains gross proceeds from the IPO of $133 million, that is excluding the green shoe option.

With nearly 35 million shares outstanding, the company is awarded a $595 million equity valuation at the offer price. This valuation includes about $125 million in net cash, which implies that operating assets are valued at around $470 million at the offer price. This marks a huge premium compared to the actual value of property, plant, and equipment on the balance sheet, listed there for some $166 million.

With the business only really starting operations in 2024, the results are very hard to read into. Pro forma revenues for the year 2024 came in at $51 million, with adjusted EBITDA reported at $18 million. First quarter sales were reported at $16.8 million, on which a $5.6 million adjusted EBITDA number was reported.

Preliminary results for the second quarter indicate revenues seen at a midpoint of $18.6 million, as growth looks relatively modest, up around 35% compared to the same period last year. So while these revenues trend around $75 per annum and positive EBITDA is reported here, the reality is that this is essentially a lease model here, with most of the investments made into GPUs, which can have wildly volatile economic implications, depending on the technological developments, among others.

In that light, it is noteworthy that the company increased the estimated economic lifetime of the GPUs to 5 years earlier this year.

Shares of the business initially moved higher to the $25 mark, but quickly fell, with underwriters later in the trading session arguably needing to defend the stock, closing down to $16.22 per share on the first day of trading, pushing down the operating asset valuation below $450 million here.

Concluding Thoughts

The risk factors in such an offering are plentiful, including, of course, the ability of the company to keep growing at a rapid pace, that of stiff competition, technological changes, debt to be incurred (given that this is a capital-intensive business), as well as the simple fact that we might be in the midst of a huge AI boom.

Furthermore, the business is a controlled one, and trends have all been very recent, making it hard to read into the financials, as much money will be spent in the coming quarters to build out the capacity. A skeptic would say that this is a pure lease model, and frankly, I lean towards that stance, which results in me seeing this as now a precious commodity supplier. This makes the situation probably solid right now, but raises questions if the AI boom or demand slows down, let alone pops.

I will be happy to watch the roll-out, but moreover, the buildout of the datacenter will continue in the coming quarters, but for now I am not enticed, even not after the softer first day trading action.

data.title: Federal Signal: Clean Up Your Portfolio With This Stock
data.description:
Company Description

Federal Signal Corporation (NYSE:FSS) designs and manufactures specialized equipment for municipal, government, industrial, and commercial clients. Additionally, some of the products sold include: sewer cleaners, street sweepers, emergency vehicle lighting, sirens, and public safety communications equipment. The company operates with two reportable segments, Environmental Solutions Group & Safety and Security Systems Group. FSS sports a current market cap of close to $7.5B and employs about 4,700 people worldwide. Over the past decade the stock has returned more than 830%, about 25% annually. Additionally, if the timeframe is shortened to just the last five years, the CAGR for Federal Signal Corp. jumps to almost 33%, significantly above the broad market.

Quality Financial Metrics

Let's take a look at some key financial metrics and assess how Federal Signal has performed.

The revenue per share for FSS has nearly tripled since 2016, rising from $11.6 per share to more than $30/share. There was consistent growth for the first four years of the chart, through 2019, with growth ranging from 10% to 20%, depending on the year. This metric declined some during the Covid year, but rebounded in FY21. Following that bounce back year, the RPS grew by 16% in FY22 & FY23, and just last year it increased again by more than 7%. I expect FSS to continue to grow its RPS metric, with revenue growth projected to be 13.35% this year, and 7.5% and 8.7% in FY26 & FY27.

The gross profit margin for Federal Signal has oscillated some over the course of the past decade. This metric was a chart low in FY21 at 23.8%; however, last fiscal year it recorded a GPM of 28.6%. Overall, the gross profit margin has risen about 10% over the past ten years. The GPM expansion has been driven by growth in aftermarket sales coupled with strategic acquisitions, most recently, Hog Technologies which closed in Q1 of this year.

The return on invested capital was on a solid upward trajectory before the pandemic hindered some of the gains this metric had made. In the few years post pandemic the ROIC remained flat, but over the last two fiscal years there has been solid growth. Typically, I prefer a ROIC around 20%, and although it's not there at this time, I believe it could eclipse that mark in the coming years. This could occur through a combination of synergies from acquisitions, like the aforementioned Hog deal, and continuing to execute on strategic growth initiatives like its "Good, Better, Best" product strategy (slide 10) where the company targets historically underserved subsegments.

Dividend Data

Federal Signal pays a quarterly dividend of $0.14, or $0.56 annually, giving it a forward dividend yield of just 0.45%. Up until recently, the company had raised its dividend on an inconsistent basis; however, over the last two years the company has raised its dividend in March, indicating signs of potential regularity. The declining payout ratio, as shown by the blue line below, is a positive sign that future dividend increases will come to fruition.

Earnings Update

About a week ago, Federal Signal announced their Q2 earnings with a double-beat. Normalized EPS was $1.17 per share, exceeding expectations by $0.11, on revenue of $565M, which also topped the consensus by almost $28M, and was a 15.3% YoY increase. Additionally, GAAP EPS was $1.16, surpassing analyst estimates by $0.10 per share.

Taking a look at the segments, the Environmental Solutions Group recorded sales of $481M, an increase of 18% year-over-year, or $72M. These results were driven by a rise in sales of road-marking and line removal equipment, along with street sweepers, partially offset by weak demand for trailers.

The Safety and Security Systems Group earned revenue of $84M, a 3% increase compared to the same time period a year ago. The only catalyst driving the growth in this portion of the company was increased demand for warning systems.

Additionally, management offered updated guidance for the year, with adjusted EPS expected between $3.92 and $4.10 per share, up from $3.63 and $3.90; indicating an approximate 20% YoY increase at the midpoint. Moreover, the revenue estimate was raised from $2.02-$2.10 billion, to $2.07-$2.13 billion, equating to approximate YoY growth of about 12%.

Overall, the earnings release was very well received by the market with shares of FSS surging almost 20% following the release. The full earnings call can be read/listened to here.

Valuation

The last piece of the puzzle is to ascertain if Federal Signal is trading for an attractive valuation, and, as always, we'll examine it from a free cash flow perspective. Also, to give a little more context on how to view the chart below, the estimated fair value is calculated by dividing the FCF/share for that year by the trailing 5-year average of the FCF yield. The discount line is 10% below the estimated FV, while the premium is 10% above, and finally, the Trim line is 40% above the approximate FV.

The custom FCF valuation model suggests FSS is trading for a moderate premium to its fair value, with a current price of about $125, and a fair price closer to $113. Amazingly, since 2016 FSS has not had a down year, although some years the stock returned a minimal amount (less than 1% twice). It's best years were 2019 where it rose almost 64%, and 2023 where it climbed more than 66%. Additionally, last year it returned more than 21% and YTD it has appreciated more than 37%. Moreover, the FCF has gone from just $0.31 per share in 2022, to $3.13 per share in 2024, following a similar path as the stock price. The FCF for FSS has seen some incredible growth in recent years, with its 5-year CAGR of around 23% and the 10-year CAGR at 14%. Using the midpoint of say 18.5% growth, we could project the FCF/share in 2025 to be $3.71 per share. Putting that into my calculations, we arrive at a FV of $112/share. FSS has been on an impressive run, it'll be interesting to see if it can continue the momentum.

FSS has a strong long-term expected rate of return of 13.48%. The pieces of that estimate are a 0.45% dividend yield, a return to fair value factor of -1.92%, and an expected earnings growth rate of almost 15%.

Final Thoughts

Federal Signal has crushed it over the past decade, performing considerably better than the overall market. The financials metrics we examined are trending higher, following a small rough patch around the pandemic. The dividend yield for FSS is incredibly low, and although the increases haven't been as consistent for as long as I'd like, I'm hopeful we'll see more predictable dividend hikes in the coming years. The earnings release last week was positive all around and the market responded accordingly with a significant boost to the stock. Ultimately, even though this stock is a little pricey right now, I think it's worth a BUY rating as the company appears to be running at full throttle.

data.title: Laureate Education: Why Its Future Deserves A Premium Valuation
data.description:
Laureate Education: A 37.2% Upside with Conservative P/E Re-Rating

Laureate Education (NASDAQ:LAUR), a U.S.-based company providing higher education in Mexico and Peru. LAUR is trading at a discounted forward P/E multiple at 13.3x, which is below the discretionary sector median of 17x and peer group average of 20.1x. I’ve done a comparative analysis and assumed a conservative forward 16.6x price-to-earnings, which I think is a fair multiple compared to its closest peers ( GHC, ATGE, LOPE). Laureate has outperformed GHC and ATGE in net margin and return on equity, which has led me to assume a forward P/E that’s close to the peers. I derived a fair price of $32.5 per share, representing an upside of 37.2%. Hence, I'm rating LAUR a Strong Buy.

An Overview of Laureate Education

Laureate Education, headquartered in Miami Florida , is an international provider of higher education, operating exclusively in Mexico and Peru. The company runs two universities in Mexico, which are Universidad del Valle de México (UVM) and Universidad Tecnológica de México (UNITEC), totaling 237K enrollment as of Q2 2025. In Peru, LAUR runs three universities, which are Universidad Peruana de Ciencias Aplicadas (UPC), Universidad Privada del Norte (UPN), and CIBERTEC, totaling 234K enrollment. Here’s a brief about each university in the slide below.

Laureate Education is identified as a Public Benefit Corp. by Delaware Division of Corporations. The company has also granted the B Corp. (Benefit Corp.) by the nonprofit organization B Lab. This certification recognizes companies meeting rigorous standards of social and environmental performance. Both designations reflect Laureate’s mission toward balancing profitability with social impact.

Financials: Growth in Peru, Decline in Mexico, Navigating FX impact

Laureate Education reported mixed signals in its Q2 2025 SEC filing. It showed revenue growth and gross margin improvement, while facing hardships in its bottom line. Revenue grew by $25MM, or roughly 5%.

The growth was driven by a 9% increase in Peru, offset by a 0.55% decrease in Mexico. LAUR enjoyed organic growth in tuition and educational services revenue of 4% in Mexico and 9.8% in Peru.

The decline in Mexico’s segment revenue was attributed to a 17.8% decrease in other revenues, though it was minimal. So, Mexico’s net sales decline of 0.55% is mainly due to discounts and waivers which countered the 4.01% growth in tuition fee revenue. Foreign currency fluctuations had a negative impact on Mexico’s segment, as the weakened Mexican peso against the U.S. dollar reduced revenue by $24.2 million. In Peru, conversely, LAUR saw a net revenue growth of 9.30%.

In contrast to the good narrative of Q2, on an HoH (Half over Half) basis, revenue witnessed a slight decline of -1.84%. The decline was due to FX loss, the sale of K-12 educational assets in Mexico impacted revenue, and increased discounts and waivers, in addition to FX loss.

My take on Laureate’s revenue is that the company is very fragile to FX fluctuations. I think the company is not able to increase the number of enrollments without offering discounts, which seems to have offset the gains from new enrollments.

Gross profit grew in dollars by 14.73%, mainly due to net change in foreign currency exchange rates that saved $20MM for LAUR, as mentioned in the 10-Q. The decrease was partially offset by $19MM incurred costs due to operational changes related to the high number of enrollment at Laureate’s institutions.

Gross Margin grew by 3.35 percentage points, as a result of Peru segment margin improved by 10.5%, and Mexico Segment margin improved by 4.9%. The margin improvement is mainly due to a favorable program mix, as revenue increased without a corresponding increase in direct costs.

Net profit fell sharply by 24%. Laureate reported a loss of $25.5MM for Q2, compared to a gain of $27.4MM in the same period in 2024. This explains the decline in both net profit and margin.

-7.12%

Outlook for Laureate Education: Balancing Expansion and Currency Volatility

The outlook for the full year is mixed, somehow on the same flow of the first half 2025. SA analysts are projecting revenue to grow by 3.82%, reaching $1.63B. I think the growth outlook will be driven by Laureate’s expansion. The company is expected to open two campuses by September 2025, which may increase the number of enrollments. Enrollments already increased through April 2025 by 7% in Mexico and 6% in Peru. According to the earnings call, the company will add two more campuses by late 2026 or early 2027, which will drive the long term revenue growth, as shown below.

Earnings per share are expected to decline for the full year, a YoY decline of 19.2%,

as a consequence of the decline trend. The decline is due to the reported GAAP net loss of $19.4MM, normalized to $10.7MM loss in Q1 2025. The company’s normalized net income in Q2 is still below what it made in the same period in 2024. Bear to mention that FX affects Laureate’s bottom line, especially the Mexican Peso fluctuations against the dollar, which would create surprises in the bottom line.

The online programs for working adults is one to watch in the coming quarters as a growing revenue generator for Laureate. This segment generally represents 22% of total revenue. It has a better margin than ordinary on-campus enrollment programs, as digital platforms or on-demand courses are lower in costs than on-campus education. The segment is already mature in Mexico, and management stated they’re applying the same model in Peru which I speculate will contribute to the stock’s future EPS growth.

Laureate has just launched the online degree programs in Peru in 2023 as it wasn’t permitted earlier.

Eilif Serck-Hanssen

“Strong interest by working adult students for that fully online product in Peru... mirroring what we saw in Mexico.”

Comparative Analysis: Time for a Re-Rating?

I compared Laureate Education to five similar companies chosen by SA quant.

LAUR is trading at a discounted forward P/E multiple of 14.3x its earnings compared to the 20.3x peer average. The stock is trading at a discounted multiple compared to four of its peers (COUR, GHC, ATGE, LOPE). I think Laureate’s multiple can go nowhere near Coursera’s, as COUR is a leading purely digital education provider. Bear mentioning Coursera might even be overvalued. However, when comparing Laureate to Graham Holdings, Adtalem Global, and Grand Canyon Education, these companies are not different from each other. Although they’re trading at a premium compared to LAUR.

Graham Holdings at a 20.8x forward P/E owns Kaplan, Inc., that offers educational services in nearly 30 countries through K-12 education, higher education.

Adtalem Education at a 15.9x forward P/E specializes in providing healthcare education, mainly in the U.S., and runs the American University in the Caribbean and Ross University in the Caribbean, which is exposed to similar geographic risks as Laureate Education. Finally, Grand Canyon Education (LOPE) at a 21.4x forward P/E offers supportive functions for higher education institutions.

Now that I have briefly explained GHC, ATGE, LOPE, I’m going to discuss why LAUR deserves a re-rate at a forward P/E near those stocks.

Laureate Education enjoys a lower level of indirect operating costs. Despite FX headwinds or tailwinds, the company’s trailing twelve months net income margin stands at 16.4%, which is higher than GHC 14.1%, ATGE 13.3%, and lower than LOPE 21.9%. Adding to the legit net margin, I expect Laureate’s expansion in the online degrees in Peru will add to its revenue with expanded margin. Moreover, Laureate enjoys a strong level of return on equity 26%, compared to GHC 16.1%, ATGE 16.6%, though slightly lower than LOPE 29%. Laureate carries a small net debt of $313MM, with a net debt to EBITDA ratio of 0.7x, meaning it could clear its debt with 70% of operating income.

Adding to the undervaluation case, Laureate is trading at 7.9x forward EV/EBITDA, which is a massive discount compared to the 12.9x peer average.

Valuation Summary

I believe that the market could re-rate Laureate at a higher P/E, conservatively speaking 16.3x, which is lower than the peer average by 4x. I chose 16.6x in respect to the geographic risks associated with Peru and Mexico. If the company doesn’t witness major surprises related to FX fluctuations, it’s positioned for a long term EPS growth outlook. The main drivers would be the new campuses that management declared that will be added, and expanding in the online degree programs in Peru. The company’s low debt doesn’t put stress on the bottom line and it doesn’t pay dividends, which would help in financing the campuses. By applying the conservative 16.6 forward P/E, multiplying by 2026 EPS $1.96, provided by SA, we derive a fair value of $32.5, targeting an upside potential of 29.1%. It usually takes the market 18-24 months to re-rate the stock. In my view, it would take LAUR to deliver the projected EPS growth to deserve the fair multiple compared to the peers.

Assumed Forward P/E

16.3x

Estimated EPS 2026

$1.96

Target Price

$31.9

Potential Upside

29.1%

Bearish Case:

As the company is purely operating in Mexico and Peru which are somewhat developing countries, it faces inflation or currency devaluation risks. FX fluctuations can substantially impact LAUR’s bottom line.

Additionally, any changes in government policies regarding for-profit higher education could influence Laureate’s enrollment rate.

Investor Takeaway:

I see LAUR as a Strong Buy at current P/E and EV/EBITDA levels, with a focus on medium to long-term holding. Key catalysts to watch would include the enrollment growth from the new campuses, capitalizing on the online degree programs in Peru. Currency volatility in Mexico and Peru remains the main factor that could disrupt earnings. Key risks would be monitoring FX trends, and any changes in education policy in both countries will be critical in assessing whether the re-rating thesis stays intact.
